name: RegexBuilder.Extra

values.name: between
values.comment:
`between i j pattern` will match `pattern` at least `i` and at most `j` times.
Invalid parameters `i`, `j` will result in empty subpatterns as shown above.

values.name: ifFollowedBy
values.comment: `a >> ifFollowedBy b` matches `a` only if it is followed by `b`, but doesn't include `b` in the match

values.name: repeat
values.comment:
Repeat a `Pattern`.
Given a 0 or a negative number, this will result in an empty subpattern:

    char 'a'
        >> repeat -1 char'b'
        >> char 'c'

    ==

    char 'a'
        >> char 'c'

values.name: wordBoundary
values.comment:
Matches at the beginning or end of a word.
Note that a word is anything consisting of some of A-Z, a-z, 0-9 and underscore.
If you are dealing with Unicode, this might not be the behavior you want.

name: RegexBuilder

aliases.name: Pattern
aliases.comment:
A `Pattern` is just a function from some internal state to another,
describing a regex in the process.
The most important part to take away from that:
You can **combine `Pattern`s using `>>`.**

    char 'a' >> char 'b' == exactly "ab"

values.name: either
values.comment:
Introduce alternatives.

    -- corresponds to the regex `a|b`
    either [char 'a', char 'b']

values.name: many
values.comment:
Matches the given `Pattern` *1 or more* times.
If you are looking for 0 or more repetitions, combine this with maybe:

    zeroOrMoreAs = maybe (many (char 'a'))

Note: When you turn this combination into a String or Regex, this will be transformed into a single "0 or more"
operator. So don't be afraid to use it like this!

values.name: noDigit
values.comment: Matches anything but a decimal digit.

values.name: noneOf
values.comment: Matches none of the given characters.

values.name: oneOf
values.comment: Matches one of the given characters.

values.name: remember
values.comment:
When working with `Match`es, you need `remember` to make subpatterns.
I recommend using this with named `Pattern`s:

    hexNumber : Pattern
    hexNumber =
        let
            hexDigit =
                either
                    [ digit
                    , oneOf [ 'A','B','C','D','E','F' ]
                    ]
        in
            exactly "0x"
                >> remember (many hexDigit)

values.name: toRegexString
values.comment:
Gives the string representation of the given `Pattern`.

    either
        [ char 'a'
        , char 'b'
        ]
        |> toRegexString
    ==
        "a|b"

values.name: whiteSpace
values.comment:
Matches a single white space character, including (but not limited to)
space, tab, form feed and line feed.
